subject: maths
title: Circles

nodes.title: Solve tangent-to-a-circle problems
nodes.brief: Apply tangent properties (perpendicularity, equal lengths) to compute lengths/angles and prove results in circle figures.
nodes.quote: In this chapter, we will study about the existence of the tangents to a circle and also study some of their properties.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify which tangent property to use.
Every tangent problem reduces to one of two properties: the radius at the contact point is perpendicular to the tangent (Theorem 10.1), or two tangents from the same external point are equal in length (Theorem 10.2). Decide which applies before drawing anything.

PART 2 — Draw radii to contact points to create right angles.
Once the relevant property is chosen, draw the radius (or radii) to each point of contact. This converts the figure into a right triangle or a pair of congruent triangles. For example, joining O to the contact point T on tangent PT immediately gives a right angle at T.

PART 3 — Set up and solve using Pythagoras, congruence, or angle sums.
With the right triangle or congruence identified, apply the appropriate tool: Pythagoras for a missing length (e.g. PT = sqrt(OP^2 - OT^2)), RHS congruence to prove equal tangents, or angle-sum in a quadrilateral to show supplementary angles.
keyMoves: Identify which tangent property applies (perpendicularity or equal lengths)
Draw radii to points of contact to create right angles
Set up a right triangle or congruence and solve
misconceptions: Trying to solve without drawing the radius to the point of contact
Assuming a tangent meets the circle at two points

nodes.title: Define a tangent to a circle
nodes.brief: A tangent is a line meeting the circle at exactly one point (the point of contact).
nodes.quote: you have seen that a tangent* to a circle is a line that intersects the circle at only one point.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Count common points to name the line.
A line in the plane of a circle can meet it at 0, 1, or 2 points. Exactly one common point means the line is a tangent; two common points means a secant; zero means non-intersecting. So to classify: count the intersections.

PART 2 — Name the single common point the point of contact.
The unique point where a tangent touches the circle is called the point of contact. It is the only point shared by the line and the circle, and a tangent never crosses into the interior of the circle.
keyMoves: Count common points of the line and circle: exactly one means tangent
Name the single common point the point of contact
misconceptions: Confusing a tangent with a secant (which meets the circle in two points)
Thinking a tangent can cross into the circle

nodes.title: Classify a line relative to a circle
nodes.brief: Decide whether a line is non-intersecting, a secant, or a tangent by its number of common points.
nodes.quote: In Fig. 10.1 (iii), there is only one point A which is common to the line PQ and the circle. In this case, the line is called a tangent to the circle.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Three cases, determined by common-point count.
Given a line and a circle in a plane, there are exactly three cases: 0 common points (the line does not touch the circle — non-intersecting), 2 common points (the line cuts through — secant), or 1 common point (the line just touches — tangent). No other case is possible.

PART 2 — Map the count to the correct name.
To classify: count the intersections. 0 → non-intersecting; 2 → secant; 1 → tangent. Example: a horizontal line below a circle has 0 points in common (non-intersecting); a horizontal line cutting through the middle has 2 (secant); a horizontal line touching the very bottom has 1 (tangent).
keyMoves: Count common points: 0, 2, or 1
Map 0 to non-intersecting, 2 to secant, 1 to tangent
misconceptions: Calling a two-point line a tangent
Believing there is a fourth possible position

nodes.title: Count tangents from a point by its location
nodes.brief: 0 tangents from inside, 1 from on the circle, exactly 2 from an external point.
nodes.quote: Case 3 : There are exactly two tangents to a circle through a point lying outside the circle.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Locate the point relative to the circle.
The number of tangents from a point depends entirely on whether the point is inside the circle, on the circle, or outside the circle. Locate the point first.

PART 2 — Map location to tangent count.
Inside: no tangent can be drawn — any line through the point crosses the circle twice (making it a secant, not a tangent). On the circle: exactly one tangent, perpendicular to the radius at that point. Outside: exactly two tangents can be drawn, touching the circle at two distinct points.
keyMoves: Locate the point: inside, on, or outside the circle
Map to 0, 1, or 2 tangents respectively
misconceptions: Saying two tangents can be drawn from a point inside the circle
Forgetting that a point on the circle admits exactly one tangent

nodes.title: Apply tangent perpendicular to radius
nodes.brief: The radius drawn to the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent there.
nodes.quote: Theorem 10.1 : The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the point of contact.
Theoem 10.1 applies at exactly the point where the tangent touches the circle — the point of contact. Identify it first before marking any angle. Any other point on the tangent line is NOT the contact point, so the perpendicularity does not apply there.

PART 2 — Draw the radius to it and mark the 90 degree angle.
Join the centre O to the point of contact T. By Theorem 10.1, angle OTP = 90 degrees (where PT is the tangent). This right angle is the starting point of every tangent calculation in the chapter — it converts the figure into a right triangle.
keyMoves: Identify the point of contact
Draw the radius to it and mark the 90 degree angle with the tangent
misconceptions: Marking the right angle at the wrong point (not the point of contact)
Assuming any radius is perpendicular to the tangent

nodes.title: Justify why a tangent point lies outside
nodes.brief: Any point on the tangent other than the contact point lies outside the circle, so its distance from the centre exceeds the radius.
nodes.quote: Therefore, OQ is longer than the radius OP of the circle. That is,
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Take any other point Q on the tangent.
The tangent line touches the circle at exactly one point P. Pick any other point Q on the tangent line (on either side of P). Because the tangent meets the circle at only P, Q is not on the circle — so Q must lie outside the circle.

PART 2 — Argue Q is outside so OQ > OP, making OP the perpendicular.
Because Q lies outside the circle, OQ > radius = OP. This holds for every Q on the tangent other than P. That means OP is the shortest distance from O to the tangent line. The shortest distance from a point to a line is always along the perpendicular, so OP is perpendicular to the tangent — which is Theorem 10.1.
keyMoves: Take any other point Q on the tangent
Argue Q is outside so OQ > OP, hence OP is shortest and perpendicular
misconceptions: Thinking points on the tangent could lie inside the circle
Confusing shortest distance with an arbitrary distance

nodes.title: Apply equal lengths of tangents from external point
nodes.brief: Two tangents drawn from the same external point to a circle are equal in length.
nodes.quote: Theorem 10.2 : The lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify two tangents from a single external point.
Theorem 10.2 applies when two tangents are drawn from the same external point P, touching the circle at two different points A and B. The point P must be external (outside the circle).

PART 2 — Set their lengths equal.
PA = PB — always, regardless of the circle's size or the exact position of P. This equality is used to create equal-length pairs around circumscribed figures (quadrilaterals, triangles with an inscribed circle) and to deduce isosceles triangles like PAB.
keyMoves: Identify two tangents from a single external point
Set their lengths equal
misconceptions: Equating tangents from different external points
Forgetting the point must be external

nodes.title: Prove equal tangents via RHS congruence
nodes.brief: Join centre to point and to contact points; right triangles are congruent by RHS, so tangents are equal by CPCT.
nodes.quote: Therefore, Δ OQP ≅ Δ ORP (RHS)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Join centre to external point and to both contact points.
Given circle O, external point P, tangents PA and PB at contact points A and B. Construct segments OA, OB, and OP. This creates two right triangles OAP and OBP (right angles at A and B by Theorem 10.1).

PART 2 — Apply RHS and CPCT to conclude PA = PB.
In triangles OAP and OBP: angle OAP = angle OBP = 90 degrees (radius perpendicular to tangent); OA = OB (radii of the same circle); OP = OP (common hypotenuse). By RHS congruence, triangle OAP is congruent to triangle OBP. Therefore PA = PB by CPCT.
keyMoves: Join centre to external point and to both contact points
Use radii equal, OP common, right angles, then RHS and CPCT
misconceptions: Using SSS/SAS where RHS is required
Forgetting to state the right angles come from Theorem 10.1

nodes.title: Compute tangent length using Pythagoras
nodes.brief: In the right triangle radius-tangent-line-to-centre, tangent length = sqrt(distance^2 - radius^2).
nodes.quote: PQ² = OP² – OQ² = OP² – OR² = PR² (As OQ = OR)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the right angle at the point of contact.
By Theorem 10.1, the radius OT is perpendicular to the tangent AT at the contact point T. So triangle OTA has a right angle at T, with hypotenuse OA (the line from the external point to the centre).

PART 2 — Apply Pythagoras with hypotenuse = centre-to-external distance.
In right triangle OTA: OA^2 = OT^2 + AT^2. Rearranging: AT^2 = OA^2 - OT^2, so AT = sqrt(OA^2 - OT^2). Substituting: tangent length = sqrt(d^2 - r^2) where d is the distance from the external point to the centre and r is the radius.

PART 3 — Solve for the tangent leg.
Example: circle with radius r = 7 cm, external point A at distance OA = 25 cm from centre O. Tangent length AT = sqrt(25^2 - 7^2) = sqrt(625 - 49) = sqrt(576) = 24 cm.
keyMoves: Identify the right angle at the point of contact
Apply Pythagoras with hypotenuse = centre-to-external distance
Solve for the tangent leg
misconceptions: Putting the right angle at the centre instead of the contact point
Adding instead of subtracting radius squared

nodes.title: Find chord of larger circle touching smaller concentric circle
nodes.brief: The chord of the larger circle tangent to the smaller is bisected at contact; use right triangle with the two radii.
nodes.quote: Now AB is a chord of the circle C₁ and OP ⊥ AB. Therefore, OP is the bisector of the chord AB, as the perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord, i.e., AP = BP
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise the chord is tangent to the inner circle so the inner radius is perpendicular.
In two concentric circles (both centred at O, radii R and r with R > r), a chord AB of the outer circle that touches the inner circle at M is tangent to the inner circle. By Theorem 10.1, the inner radius OM is perpendicular to AB at M.

PART 2 — Use perpendicular-from-centre-bisects-chord to halve the chord.
Since OM is perpendicular to chord AB (of the outer circle), it bisects AB by the chord-bisection theorem. So AM = BM = half the chord length.

PART 3 — Apply Pythagoras with the two radii.
In right triangle OMA: OA^2 = OM^2 + AM^2, so AM = sqrt(R^2 - r^2). Full chord = AB = 2 * sqrt(R^2 - r^2). Example: R = 10, r = 6 gives AM = sqrt(100-36) = 8, chord = 16 cm.
keyMoves: Recognise the chord is tangent to the inner circle so inner radius is perpendicular
Use perpendicular-from-centre-bisects-chord to halve the chord
Apply Pythagoras with the two radii
misconceptions: Not halving the chord before applying Pythagoras
Mixing up which radius is the hypotenuse

nodes.title: Use perpendicular from centre bisects the chord
nodes.brief: A line from the centre perpendicular to a chord bisects that chord.
nodes.quote: OP is the bisector of the chord AB, as the perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Confirm the segment from the centre is perpendicular to the chord.
This theorem (a Class 9 result) applies only when the segment from the centre meets the chord at a right angle. Verify the perpendicularity first — not every segment from the centre bisects the chord, only the perpendicular one.

PART 2 — Conclude the two half-chords are equal.
Once perpendicularity is confirmed, the chord is split into two equal halves. If M is the foot of the perpendicular from O to chord AB, then AM = MB. This gives you a half-chord length to use in the Pythagoras calculation.
keyMoves: Confirm the segment from the centre is perpendicular to the chord
Conclude the two half-chords are equal
misconceptions: Assuming any segment from the centre bisects the chord without perpendicularity
Bisecting the wrong segment

refreshers.answer: A chord of a circle is a straight line segment whose two endpoints both lie on the circle. In other words, you pick two points on the circle and join them, and that join is a chord. A chord stays inside the circle except for its two endpoints, which are on the circle. The longest possible chord is the one that passes through the centre, and that special chord is called the diameter; its length is twice the radius. Every other chord is shorter than the diameter. Be careful not to confuse a chord with an arc: the arc is the curved part of the circle between the two points, while the chord is the straight segment between them. This idea comes from Class 9 circle geometry. It is needed here because a chord of the outer circle, and the rule about how a perpendicular from the centre cuts a chord in half, are central to these tangent problems.
ladder: Mark any two points on the rim of a circle. Now join them with a straight line segment. What have you drawn?
That segment, with both ends on the circle, is a chord. Does it have to pass through the centre?
What special name does a chord get when it DOES pass through the centre?
refreshers.trigger: Student does not recall what a chord is when a chord of a circle first appears.
refreshers.returnCue: With chord clear, recall that a perpendicular dropped from the centre splits a chord into two equal halves.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: This step talks about a chord of the circle. What exactly is a chord?

refreshers.trigger: Student is shaky on what perpendicular or a right angle (90 degrees) means when this step asks them to confirm the segment from the centre meets the chord at a right angle before concluding it bisects the chord.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Quick check before we use this rule: this step says the segment from the centre must be perpendicular to the chord. What does perpendicular mean, and what angle is that?
ladder: Look at the corner of this page or a window frame. How many degrees is that square corner?

That square corner is a right angle, exactly 90 degrees, and two lines that meet making such a corner are called perpendicular. Can you point to a perpendicular pair around you, like the edges of a door?
Now look at the segment running from the centre O down to the chord AB. Does it land on the chord making that same square-corner, 90-degree angle?
If it does meet the chord at 90 degrees, the rule applies; if it slants in at some other angle, it does not. Why does it matter to check this first?
refreshers.answer: Two lines or segments are perpendicular when they meet at a right angle, an angle of exactly 90 degrees, the shape of a square corner. We mark it with a small square drawn in the corner. A right angle is one quarter of a full turn, since 360 degrees divided by 4 is 90 degrees. This is basic Class 6 to 9 geometry. It matters here because the chord-bisection rule is fussy: ONLY the segment from the centre that hits the chord at a true 90-degree angle splits the chord into two equal halves. A segment from the centre that slants in at any other angle does NOT bisect the chord. So before you claim AM equals MB, you must first confirm that the segment OM meets the chord at a right angle.
refreshers.returnCue: Good. Now that you can confirm that 90-degree angle, recall the rule: the perpendicular from the centre splits the chord into two equal halves.

nodes.title: Relate angle between tangents to central angle
nodes.brief: Angle between two tangents from an external point plus the central angle of the contact chord equals 180 degrees.
nodes.quote: Prove that the angle between the two tangents drawn from an external point to a circle is supplementary to the angle subtended by the line-segment joining the points of contact at the centre.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form the quadrilateral with centre, two contact points, and external point.
From external point T, draw two tangents TP and TQ touching the circle (centre O) at P and Q. Draw OP and OQ. The four points O, P, T, Q form a quadrilateral OPTQ.

PART 2 — Mark the two right angles at the contact points.
By Theorem 10.1, angle OPT = 90 degrees and angle OQT = 90 degrees (radius perpendicular to tangent at each contact point). Two of the four interior angles of the quadrilateral are already 90 degrees each.

PART 3 — Use angle sum 360 degrees to conclude the remaining two angles are supplementary.
Sum of interior angles of a quadrilateral = 360 degrees. So angle PTQ + angle POQ = 360 - 90 - 90 = 180 degrees. The angle between the tangents and the central angle are supplementary.
keyMoves: Form quadrilateral: centre, two contact points, external point
Mark the two right angles at the contact points
Use angle sum 360 to get the two remaining angles supplementary
misconceptions: Thinking the two angles are equal rather than supplementary
Forgetting the two right angles from the radii

nodes.title: Prove angle PTQ equals twice angle OPQ
nodes.brief: Using isosceles tangent triangle and the right angle at the contact point, derive PTQ = 2·OPQ.
nodes.quote: ∠ OPQ = ∠ OPT – ∠ TPQ = 90° – (90° – ½ θ) = ½ θ = ½ ∠ PTQ
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Set PTQ = theta and use equal tangents for isosceles base angles.
Let angle PTQ = theta. Since TP = TQ (equal tangents from T), triangle TPQ is isosceles with apex angle theta. Each base angle is (180 - theta)/2 = 90 - theta/2. So angle TPQ = angle TQP = 90 - theta/2.

PART 2 — Use angle OPT = 90 degrees from the radius.
By Theorem 10.1, the radius OP is perpendicular to the tangent PT at P, so angle OPT = 90 degrees.

PART 3 — Subtract to get OPQ = theta/2.
Angle OPQ = angle OPT - angle TPQ = 90 - (90 - theta/2) = theta/2. Therefore angle PTQ = theta = 2 * (theta/2) = 2 * angle OPQ.
keyMoves: Set PTQ = theta and use equal tangents for isosceles base angles
Use angle OPT = 90 from the radius
Subtract to get OPQ = theta/2
misconceptions: Forgetting the triangle is isosceles
Misplacing the right angle OPT

nodes.title: Compute base angles of isosceles tangent triangle
nodes.brief: In an isosceles triangle each base angle is (180 - apex)/2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the equal sides and the apex angle.
In an isosceles triangle the two sides that are equal are the legs; the angle between them (opposite the unequal base) is the apex angle. Identify these before computing. For the tangent triangle TPQ, the equal sides are the tangent segments TP = TQ, so T is the apex.

PART 2 — Compute each base angle as (180 - apex)/2.
All three angles sum to 180 degrees. The two base angles are equal, so each base angle = (180 - apex angle)/2. Example: if angle PTQ = 60 degrees, each base angle = (180 - 60)/2 = 60 degrees. If angle PTQ = 100 degrees, each base angle = (180 - 100)/2 = 40 degrees.
keyMoves: Identify the equal sides and apex angle
Compute each base angle as (180 - apex)/2
misconceptions: Dividing the wrong angle by two
Forgetting base angles are equal

refreshers.trigger: Student is about to compute base angles via (180 - apex)/2 but does not recall that the three interior angles of any triangle sum to 180 degrees, where the 180 in the formula comes from.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Quick one before we divide by two: where do you think that 180 in the formula comes from? What do the three angles of a triangle always add up to?
ladder: Sketch any triangle you like and tear off, or just imagine tearing off, its three corners. If you lay those three corner angles side by side along a straight line, what shape do they make together?
They fit exactly onto a straight line, and a straight line is a half turn. How many degrees is a straight angle, a half turn?
So no matter how you stretch or squash a triangle, its three angles always total that same amount. What is the total?
In our isosceles triangle the apex angle is one of those three, and the two equal base angles are the other two. If you know the apex and the three add to 180, how would you find one base angle?
refreshers.answer: The three interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180 degrees, no matter the triangle's shape or size. You can see it by tearing off the three corners and laying them side by side: they line up perfectly along a straight line, and a straight angle is 180 degrees. This is the angle sum property of a triangle from Class 7 to 8 geometry. Here it is exactly where the 180 in our formula comes from: the apex angle plus the two equal base angles must total 180, so the two base angles together take up (180 minus apex), and since they are equal, each one is (180 minus apex) divided by 2.
refreshers.returnCue: Good, that 180 is the angle sum of the triangle. Now subtract the apex and split what remains in half to get each base angle.

refreshers.trigger: Student does not recall that in an isosceles triangle the two angles opposite the equal sides are equal, which this node relies on to split (180 - apex) evenly between the two base angles.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Quick one before we divide by two: in a triangle with two equal sides, what do you know about the two angles down at the base?
ladder: Picture a triangle that has two sides of exactly the same length, like a tent or the letter A standing up. Does it look lopsided, or does it look perfectly balanced left to right?
Because the two long sides match, the whole shape is a mirror image of itself down the middle. If you folded it along that middle line, would the two bottom corners land right on top of each other?
Folding one bottom corner onto the other means those two angles are the same size. So in a triangle with two equal sides, what can you say about the two angles sitting opposite those equal sides?
refreshers.answer: In an isosceles triangle, the two sides that are equal force the two angles opposite them, the base angles, to be equal as well. You can see it from the symmetry: the triangle is a perfect mirror image about the line down its middle, so the two base angles fold exactly onto each other and must be the same size. This is the Class 9 isosceles triangle property, angles opposite equal sides are equal. It is exactly what lets us divide here: in triangle TPQ the tangent segments TP and TQ are equal, so the two base angles at P and Q are equal, and that is why we can take whatever is left after the apex, (180 - apex), and split it evenly into two equal halves rather than guessing how to share it.
refreshers.returnCue: Good, so the two base angles being equal is what justifies the divide-by-two. Back to it: each base angle is (180 - apex)/2.

nodes.title: Use equal tangent segments in a circumscribed quadrilateral
nodes.brief: For a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle, equal tangent segments give AB + CD = AD + BC.
nodes.quote:
A quadrilateral ABCD is drawn to circumscribe a circle (see Fig. 10.12). Prove that

AB + CD = AD + BC
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Label the four contact points and pair equal tangent segments at each vertex.
Let the circle touch AB at P, BC at Q, CD at R, and DA at S. From Theorem 10.2, tangents from each vertex are equal: AP = AS (from A), BP = BQ (from B), CQ = CR (from C), DR = DS (from D).

PART 2 — Add side lengths and group equal segments.
Express each side as a sum of two tangent segments: AB = AP + PB = AS + BQ; CD = CR + RD = CQ + DS. Adding: AB + CD = (AS + BQ) + (CQ + DS).

PART 3 — Conclude AB + CD = AD + BC.
Regroup: (AS + DS) + (BQ + CQ) = AD + BC. So AB + CD = AD + BC.
keyMoves: Label the four points of contact and pair equal tangent segments at each vertex
Add side lengths and group equal segments
Conclude AB + CD = AD + BC
misconceptions: Equating tangent segments from different vertices
Mislabelling which segments share a vertex

nodes.title: Find triangle sides from inscribed-circle tangent segments
nodes.brief: Use equal tangent segments from each vertex of a triangle with an inscribed circle to find unknown sides.
nodes.quote: A triangle ABC is drawn to circumscribe a circle of radius 4 cm such that the segments BD and DC into which BC is divided by the point of contact D are of lengths 8 cm and 6 cm respectively (see Fig. 10.14). Find the sides AB and AC.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Name the contact points on the three sides.
Let the incircle touch BC at D, CA at E, and AB at F. These are the three points of contact, one on each side of the triangle.

PART 2 — Set the two tangents from each vertex equal.
From Theorem 10.2: BF = BD (tangents from B), CD = CE (tangents from C), AE = AF (tangents from A). If BD = 8 and CD = 6, then BF = 8 and CE = 6. Let AE = AF = a.

PART 3 — Add segment pairs to get each side.
AB = AF + FB = a + 8; BC = BD + DC = 8 + 6 = 14; CA = CE + EA = 6 + a. Use additional information (such as radius and area formula) to find a, then compute AB and AC. For the NCERT example with radius 4: a = 7, so AB = 15 cm and AC = 13 cm.
keyMoves: Name contact points on the three sides
Set the two tangents from each vertex equal
Add segment pairs to get each side
misconceptions: Pairing tangent segments from different vertices
Forgetting a side is the sum of two distinct vertex tangents

nodes.title: Use similar right triangles to find a tangent length
nodes.brief: Set up AA-similar right triangles in a tangent-chord figure and use the proportion to find a length.
nodes.quote: Therefore, right triangle TRP is similar to the right triangle PRO by AA similarity.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Establish the two right triangles share an equal acute angle.
In a tangent-secant figure from external point T, draw tangent TP and secant TAB. In triangles TPA and TBP: angle T is common to both. A second equal angle pair comes from the tangent-chord angle relationship. Both triangles have angle T in common, making them AA-similar.

PART 2 — Conclude AA similarity and write the proportion.
Triangles TPA and TBP are AA-similar. The proportion of corresponding sides gives: TP/TA = TB/TP, which simplifies to TP^2 = TA * TB (the power-of-a-point relation).

PART 3 — Solve for the unknown length.
If TA and TB are known, TP = sqrt(TA * TB). If TP and one segment are known, find the other. Example: TP = 15 cm, TA = 9 cm gives TB = TP^2/TA = 225/9 = 25 cm.
keyMoves: Establish the two right triangles share an equal acute angle
Conclude AA similarity
Write the proportion of corresponding sides and solve
misconceptions: Pairing non-corresponding sides in the proportion
Assuming similarity without justifying the equal angle

nodes.title: Prove tangents at diameter ends are parallel
nodes.brief: Both tangents are perpendicular to the same diameter, so they are parallel.
nodes.quote: Prove that the tangents drawn at the ends of a diameter of a circle are parallel.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Mark each tangent perpendicular to the diameter at its endpoint.
Let AB be a diameter. The tangent at A is perpendicular to OA (= AB) by Theorem 10.1. The tangent at B is perpendicular to OB (= AB) by Theorem 10.1. So both tangents are perpendicular to the line AB.

PART 2 — Conclude using the parallel-lines criterion.
Two lines that are both perpendicular to the same line are parallel to each other. Since both tangents are perpendicular to diameter AB, the two tangents are parallel.
keyMoves: Mark the tangent at each diameter end perpendicular to the diameter
Use 'two lines perpendicular to a common line are parallel'
misconceptions: Forgetting the diameter is the common perpendicular line
Assuming the tangents meet

nodes.title: Solve a right triangle with the Pythagoras theorem
nodes.brief: Relate the three sides of a right triangle by hypotenuse² = leg² + leg².
nodes.quote: Also, OR = √(OP² – PR²) = √(5² – 4²) cm = 3 cm.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle).
In a right triangle, the hypotenuse is always the longest side and is opposite the right angle. Label it c; the two legs are a and b. The relation is c^2 = a^2 + b^2.

PART 2 — Rearrange to find an unknown leg.
To find leg a when c and b are known: a^2 = c^2 - b^2, so a = sqrt(c^2 - b^2). Example: hypotenuse = 13, one leg = 5. Other leg = sqrt(13^2 - 5^2) = sqrt(169 - 25) = sqrt(144) = 12.
keyMoves: Identify the hypotenuse (longest side, opposite the right angle)
Form leg² = hyp² - other-leg² and take the square root
misconceptions: Treating a leg as the hypotenuse
Forgetting to take the square root

refreshers.trigger: Student does not recall what a square root is or how to undo a square, e.g. that sqrt(144) = 12, when the Pythagoras step needs sqrt(c^2 - b^2).
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Quick one before we finish this triangle: the relation gives us the side SQUARED, so what do we do to a number to undo the squaring and get the side itself?
ladder: You already know squaring: 12 squared means 12 times 12. What is 12 times 12?
So squaring took 12 up to 144. Taking a square root is just the reverse: it asks, what number times itself gives 144? What is that number?
Try one more: what number times itself gives 25? That number is the square root of 25.
So if you ever land on side-length-squared = 144, the side itself is the square root of 144. What is it?
refreshers.answer: The square root of a number is the value that, multiplied by itself, gives that number. Squaring and taking a square root are opposite moves: since 12 times 12 = 144, the square root of 144 (written sqrt(144)) is 12, because 12 undoes the squaring. We write it with the sqrt sign, and for the side of a triangle we only want the positive root, since a length is never negative. You met squares and square roots in Class 8. It matters right here because Pythagoras gives you the side SQUARED, like a^2 = c^2 - b^2 = 144, and that is not the answer yet: you must take the square root to un-square it, sqrt(144) = 12, to get the actual side length. Skipping that last step leaves you with the squared value, not the side.
refreshers.returnCue: Good, so once you have leg-squared = c^2 - b^2, take the square root to get the leg itself. Back to our triangle.

nodes.title: Apply RHS congruence and CPCT
nodes.brief: Two right triangles with equal hypotenuse and one equal side are congruent (RHS); corresponding parts are then equal (CPCT).
nodes.quote: This gives PQ = PR (CPCT)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Verify a right angle, equal hypotenuses, and one equal side.
RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) applies to right triangles: both triangles must have a right angle, their hypotenuses must be equal, and one additional pair of sides must be equal. Check all three conditions before applying the rule.

PART 2 — Declare RHS congruence, then extract equal parts by CPCT.
Once RHS is established, the triangles are congruent and all corresponding parts are equal. CPCT (Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles) lets you conclude that any pair of corresponding sides or angles are equal — for example, in the equal-tangents proof, CPCT gives PA = PB.
keyMoves: Verify a right angle, equal hypotenuses, and one equal side
Declare RHS congruence
Extract the needed equal parts by CPCT
misconceptions: Applying RHS without a right angle present
Claiming CPCT before congruence is established

narrative:
## What I read (as reviewer)

I re-read the full segmented chapter `jemh110.txt` (NCERT Class 10 Maths, Ch.10 "Circles") — SEG 0 Introduction, SEG 1 Tangent to a Circle, SEG 2 Exercise 10.1, SEG 3 Number of Tangents from a Point, SEG 4 Exercise 10.2, SEG 5 Summary — plus all three worked Examples. I cross-checked every exam-sourced gate against `_maths-exams.txt` (Standard SQP Q3/Q7/Q33; Basic SQP Q5/Q22) at the exact line offsets.

## Corrections I made to the draft

1. **Citation sec error (D-120) — `relate-angle-tangents-central-angle`.** The node quote ("Prove that the angle between the two tangents drawn from an external point...is supplementary to the angle subtended...at the centre.") is Exercise 10.2 Q10, which lives in **SEG 4**, not SEG 3. The draft tagged sec 3. Verified verbatim at the Exercise 10.2 segment and reset sec → 4.

2. **Gate self-consistency error — Exercise 10.1 Q3 tangent length.** The draft made this a `numeric` gate with value **119**, but the true length is PQ = √(12² − 5²) = √119 ≈ 10.91, NOT 119 (119 is the radicand, not the length). The source only states the answer as option "(D) √119 cm". Converted to an **mcq** gate keyed to "√119 cm" so the deterministic grader passes against a true canonical answer.

3. **Too-short / unlocatable gate quotes replaced with ≥6-word verbatim spans.** Several gate quotes were either fabricated option lines that don't sit in the segment, or 3-word fragments that fail a meaningful substring check:
   - Basic SQP Q22 gate quote was "= 3 cm" (the √ marking-scheme line carries multibyte glyphs and is only 3 tokens). Replaced with the verbatim question line "In two concentric circles, a chord of length 8 cm of the larger circle touches the" (line 918).
   - Exercise 10.2 Q1 radius gate quote was an options line "(A) 7 cm ..."; replaced with the verbatim Q1 statement "From a point Q, the length of the tangent to a circle is 24 cm and the distance of Q from the centre is 25 cm. The radius of the circle is".
   - Exercise 10.2 Q2 angle gate quote was an options line; replaced with the verbatim Q2 statement "if TP and TQ are the two tangents to a circle with centre O so that ∠ POQ = 110°, then ∠ PTQ is equal to".
   These keep the same answer keys (7, 70, 3) which I re-derived and confirmed.

4. **Tightened the Standard SQP Q33 BR gate prompt** to drop a stray contradictory CQ value and match the marking-scheme chain (AR=AQ=7, AB=10 ⇒ BR=3). Quote unchanged ("BP = BR = AB-AR = 3cm", verbatim line 570).

5. **Added a second gate on `apply-equal-tangent-lengths`** (it previously shared one mcq) keyed to the verbatim proof line "We are required to prove that PQ = PR." — and trimmed that node's other gate prompt to match its quote.

## Verified-and-kept

- Every other chapter node quote located verbatim in its tagged SEG (Theorems 10.1/10.2, the RHS/CPCT proof lines, Example 1/2/3 lines, Case 3, the two Exercise 10.1 fill-ins).
- Every numeric answer re-derived: Q1→7, Q2→70, Q6→3, Q7→8, Q8(AD)→3, Q3(goal)→50, Std Q7→140, Std Q33→8 and 3, Basic Q5→145, Basic Q22→3, Example 2→30, Example 3→6.6667, isosceles base→70, Pythagoras OR→3.
- **Graph:** single root `solve-circle-tangent-problems` (only ever a parent); 20 nodes, all appearing in ≥1 edge (no orphans); every edge endpoint is a defined slug; acyclic (all dependency edges point from application skills down to the two property pillars and bedrock tools). 100% node→gate coverage (every node has ≥1 gate).

## Grain

Grain looks correct: each node is a single failable move. No pure-exposition node survived (the pulley/wheel/bicycle activities, the Thomas Fineke etymology, and Activity 1–3 manipulatives are exposition and remain dropped). I did not split further — the chapter genuinely teaches two theorems plus a fan of application skills, which the draft already separates.

## Uncertainty

The exam marking-scheme glyphs (e.g. "7. C) 140𝑂 1", "5. D) 145∘ 1") contain non-ASCII math characters; I kept those gate quotes byte-for-byte as they appear so the substring check matches the source. Example 3's TP = 20/3 is expressed numerically as 6.6667 (tol 0.01).
absentNotes: The chapter does NOT teach (so omitted per retrieve-don't-generate): (1) compass/straightedge construction of tangents from an external point — that is the Constructions chapter; this chapter only states the number-of-tangents result. (2) Tangent-chord / alternate-segment theorem — not in NCERT Class 10. (3) The "centre lies on the bisector of the angle between the two tangents" is only a Remark (folded into the proof skills, not a standalone node). (4) History/etymology of "tangent" (Thomas Fineke, 1583), the pulley/cart-wheel/bicycle motivational passages, and Activities 1–3 manipulatives are pure exposition and were dropped. (5) Power-of-a-point / tangent-secant length product — not taught. (6) Coordinate-geometry treatment of tangents — absent. (7) The full formal write-ups of the "prove" exercises (Q9 ∠AOB=90°, Q11 rhombus, Q13 supplementary opposite sides) are represented by their underlying property skills (equal-tangents, perpendicular-radius) rather than as separate proof nodes, since each reduces to the two core properties already captured; only the two genuinely distinct provable patterns the exercises introduce — `relate-angle-tangents-central-angle` (Q10) and `prove-tangents-diameter-parallel` (Q4) — were kept as their own nodes. (8) Class IX prerequisites (definition of circle/chord/segment/sector/arc) are referenced but not taught here; only `use-perpendicular-bisects-chord`, which is actively reused in Examples 1 and 3, was retained as bedrock.
